target.title: fix(cli): honor ZDOTDIR/XDG_CONFIG_HOME in completion install (#63069)
target.body:
## Summary

Shell completion install/check helpers hardcoded zsh, fish, and bash profile paths under `$HOME`, ignoring `$ZDOTDIR` and `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and disagreeing on bash `.bashrc` vs `.bash_profile`. Reporter on macOS / zsh with `$ZDOTDIR=$HOME/.config/zsh` saw completion silently written to `$HOME/.zshrc` (a file the shell never reads), so completion never activates in new shells. This PR centralizes profile-path resolution behind a single resolver that honors both env vars, treats `.bashrc`/`.bash_profile` as a priority-ordered candidate list, and lets the check and install paths agree on the same file.

## Root Cause

- `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts:129` (before): `getShellProfilePath()` returned `path.join(home, ".zshrc")`, `path.join(home, ".bashrc")`, `path.join(home, ".config", "fish", "config.fish")` regardless of `$ZDOTDIR` / `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME`.
- `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts:151` (before): `isCompletionInstalled()` called `getShellProfilePath(shell)` (HOME-only) and returned `false` for bash even when the install actually wrote to `.bash_profile`.
- `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts:215` (before): `installCompletion()` separately hardcoded `path.join(home, ".zshrc")`, `path.join(home, ".bashrc")` / `.bash_profile` fallback, and `path.join(home, ".config", "fish", "config.fish")` — so when `.bashrc` was absent, install wrote to `.bash_profile` while check still looked at `.bashrc`, causing repeated `Enable completion?` prompts on every `openclaw update`.
- Execution path: `setup` / `update` / `completion --install` → `installCompletion()` writes to one path → `isCompletionInstalled()` (next session) checks the OTHER path → both report `false` so install is offered again, and the previously written source line lives in a file the shell never reads.
- clawsweeper review on #63069: *"Queue a focused fix PR because the report is valid, the affected files and tests are clear, and the target-repo implementation PR is closed unmerged."* and *"A shared resolver used by both status and install paths is the narrowest maintainable fix."*

## Changes

- `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts`: introduce `resolveShellProfilePathCandidates(shell, env, homeDir)` that returns candidate paths in priority order — `$ZDOTDIR/.zshrc` then `$HOME/.zshrc` for zsh, `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/fish/config.fish` then `$HOME/.config/fish/config.fish` for fish, `.bashrc` then `.bash_profile` for bash. Make `getShellProfilePath()` async, return the first existing candidate (or the first candidate as the canonical install target when none exist). Update `isCompletionInstalled` and `usesSlowDynamicCompletion` to `await` it. Drop the duplicate hardcoded paths in `installCompletion` and route entirely through `getShellProfilePath`, so check and install always agree on the same file.
- `src/cli/completion-runtime.profile-path.test.ts` (new): 12 colocated regression tests covering `$ZDOTDIR` zsh priority, `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` fish priority, bash `.bashrc → .bash_profile` order, whitespace-only `$ZDOTDIR` handling, `os.homedir()` fallback when `env.HOME` is unset, and live `isCompletionInstalled()` against real temp directories where the OpenClaw marker lives in the non-default file.

Net diff: **+237 / −36 LOC across 2 files** (1 new test file + 1 production file).

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: macOS / zsh users with `$ZDOTDIR=$HOME/.config/zsh` (a common dotfile layout, often via `/etc/zshenv` or `.zshenv`) see `openclaw completion --install` write the source line to `$HOME/.zshrc` instead of `$ZDOTDIR/.zshrc`, so completion never loads in their shell. Same class of bug applies to fish users with `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` set, and to bash users who only have `~/.bash_profile` (macOS default): `isCompletionInstalled()` checks `.bashrc` while `installCompletion()` falls back to `.bash_profile`, so every `openclaw update` re-asks `Enable completion?`. Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/63069
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-65656` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/cli/completion-runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, slices the patched `resolveShellProfilePathCandidates` + `getShellProfilePath` block (offset-pinned), prints its SHA-256, then runs both functions against (a) synthetic env tables matching the reporter scenarios and (b) real temp directories on disk where the OpenClaw marker is dropped into the non-default file. No mocks. PR head: `5c935bf8` on `MoerAI:fix/shell-completion-xdg-zdotdir`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/shell-completion-xdg-zdotdir && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr63069.mts`. The smoke uses `fs.mkdtemp` to build a real `$ZDOTDIR/.zshrc` and `$HOME/.zshrc` pair, drops the `# OpenClaw Completion` marker into the `$ZDOTDIR` one only, and asks `getShellProfilePath("zsh", {HOME, ZDOTDIR})` which file it picks. Same for the macOS bash case where only `.bash_profile` exists.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `5c935bf8` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, patched resolver block 1961 bytes, SHA-256 `6d240bd88d09e8184ee26f26f4b7336d5a42940ecba6ce63e00e7fd65a31d3e6`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr63069.mts
=== Patched profile-path resolver — bytes from src/cli/completion-runtime.ts ===
offset: 4838 -> 6799 ( 1961 bytes)
SHA-256: 6d240bd88d09e8184ee26f26f4b7336d5a42940ecba6ce63e00e7fd65a31d3e6

=== Scenario A: ZDOTDIR set, $ZDOTDIR/.zshrc preferred ===
candidates: [ '\\home\\example\\.config\\zsh\\.zshrc', '\\home\\example\\.zshrc' ]

=== Scenario B: ZDOTDIR unset, falls back to $HOME/.zshrc ===
candidates: [ '\\home\\example\\.zshrc' ]

=== Scenario C: bash .bashrc + .bash_profile (macOS fallback) ===
candidates: [ '\\home\\example\\.bashrc', '\\home\\example\\.bash_profile' ]

=== Scenario D: XDG_CONFIG_HOME set, fish preferred ===
candidates: [
  '\\home\\example\\.alt-config\\fish\\config.fish',
  '\\home\\example\\.config\\fish\\config.fish'
]

=== Scenario E: getShellProfilePath picks the first EXISTING candidate (ZDOTDIR wins) ===
zdotdir zshrc exists: true
home zshrc exists: true
getShellProfilePath returned: C:\Users\pss\AppData\Local\Temp\openclaw-pr63069-VIFFw8\.config\zsh\.zshrc
matches zdotdir candidate: true

=== Scenario F: macOS bash with only .bash_profile (no .bashrc) ===
.bashrc exists: false
.bash_profile exists: true
getShellProfilePath returned: C:\Users\pss\AppData\Local\Temp\openclaw-pr63069-bash-Aryipc\.bash_profile
matches .bash_profile: true

=== Pass/fail summary ===
  PASS  ZDOTDIR pref
  PASS  ZDOTDIR fallback
  PASS  no ZDOTDIR -> .zshrc
  PASS  bash .bashrc first
  PASS  bash .bash_profile 2nd
  PASS  fish XDG pref
  PASS  fish fallback
  PASS  picks existing zdotdir zshrc
  PASS  macOS picks .bash_profile w/o .bashrc
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The resolver returns `$ZDOTDIR/.zshrc` first when `ZDOTDIR` is set (Scenario A), `$HOME/.zshrc` when it is not (Scenario B), `.bashrc` then `.bash_profile` for bash (Scenario C), and `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/fish/config.fish` first when set (Scenario D). The live filesystem tests confirm `getShellProfilePath("zsh", {HOME, ZDOTDIR})` actually picks the existing `$ZDOTDIR` file when both candidates exist on disk (Scenario E, this directly satisfies the reporter's macOS / zsh / `$ZDOTDIR=$HOME/.config/zsh` setup), and falls through to `.bash_profile` on a macOS-style bash setup where only that file exists (Scenario F, this satisfies the bash `.bashrc`/`.bash_profile` check/install agreement bug). All 9 assertions PASS against the patched resolver pinned by SHA-256 `6d240bd88d09e8184ee26f26f4b7336d5a42940ecba6ce63e00e7fd65a31d3e6`.
- What was not tested: An interactive `openclaw onboard` / `openclaw update` flow was not exercised on the contributor machine. The fix is a pure path-resolution change inside the existing check/install helpers; the resolver returns plain strings that flow into `fs.readFile` / `fs.writeFile` unchanged, so the only behavioral change is the file path picked. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live macOS interactive smoke is required; the reporter's issue already contains a concrete `$ZDOTDIR=~/.config/zsh` reproducer.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/cli/completion-runtime.profile-path.test.ts` — 12 new colocated regression cases (ZDOTDIR pref/fallback, whitespace-only ZDOTDIR, XDG fish pref/fallback, bash candidate order, os.homedir() fallback, plus 4 live-tempdir `isCompletionInstalled` cases covering ZDOTDIR priority, `.bash_profile`-only macOS, `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` fish, and the no-profile false case).
- `pnpm test src/cli/completion-cli.test.ts src/cli/completion-cli.write-state.test.ts src/wizard/setup.completion.test.ts` — unchanged, exercise the broader completion CLI; the resolver refactor preserves the original behavior when neither `ZDOTDIR` nor `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is set and `.bashrc` exists, so existing tests continue to pass.

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix for #63069. `installCompletion()` no longer duplicates the per-shell path logic; both the check path (`isCompletionInstalled`, `usesSlowDynamicCompletion`) and the install path now share `getShellProfilePath()`, so `.bashrc` vs `.bash_profile` and `$ZDOTDIR`/`$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` stay consistent.
- Compatibility: when neither env var is set and `.bashrc` exists, the resolver returns exactly the same paths as the previous implementation, so existing installs are unaffected. Powershell paths are unchanged.
- Out of scope: `src/commands/doctor-completion.ts:111` and `:161` print hardcoded reload-hint strings (`source ~/.zshrc` etc.). Those are info-only messages on the success path and not on the failing `isCompletionInstalled` / `installCompletion` arithmetic; a separate cosmetic follow-up can route them through the same resolver.
- This follows the conservative direction in clawsweeper's review on #63069: *"A shared resolver used by both status and install paths is the narrowest maintainable fix; the closed target PR and cross-repo fork PR are useful source context, but the target repository still needs the change on main."*

Closes #63069

target.title: [codex] Add Control UI gateway request timeouts
target.body:
Summary
- Add client-side request timeouts to the Control UI gateway RPC path.
- Keep longer server-declared timeouts alive with grace while timing out unanswered default requests.
- Record bounded CLIENT_TIMEOUT timing and clear timers on responses/flushes.

Verification at current head
- Current head SHA: 95659193b66d613b5d7587f054260f8d87e12298
- CI=true pnpm test ui/src/ui/gateway.node.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
- CI=true pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ui/src/ui/gateway.ts ui/src/ui/gateway.node.test.ts
- git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD -- ui/src/ui/gateway.ts ui/src/ui/gateway.node.test.ts
- pnpm changed:lanes --base upstream/main --head HEAD --json
- CI=true pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main --head HEAD

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI Gateway RPC client requests now have bounded client-side timeouts, clear timeout timers after Gateway responses, and emit request timing without request params. This proof covers both the successful real Gateway request path and the central unanswered-open-WebSocket timeout path.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw Gateway started from current head SHA 95659193b66d613b5d7587f054260f8d87e12298 on loopback with isolated local state and token auth; real `ui/src/ui/gateway.ts` `GatewayBrowserClient` connected through an actual WebSocket transport using browser-equivalent Origin plus WebCrypto device identity/signing. Additional timeout-specific proof used the same real `GatewayBrowserClient` against an actual local `ws` WebSocketServer that completed `hello-ok`, kept the socket open, and intentionally never answered one request.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started `pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 18977 --bind loopback --auth token --token <redacted> --compact --cli-backend-logs`; checked `curl http://127.0.0.1:18977/healthz`; checked `HOME=<isolated> pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:18977 --token <redacted> --json`; ran a Node/tsx browser-shaped harness importing `GatewayBrowserClient` from `ui/src/ui/gateway.ts` and requesting `health` with a 5000ms timeout. Then ran a second Node/tsx browser-shaped no-response harness at the same head SHA: actual `GatewayBrowserClient`, actual local `ws` WebSocketServer, Origin `http://127.0.0.1:18988`, generated WebCrypto device identity, `connect` answered with `hello-ok`, and `proof.neverResponds` deliberately left unanswered with `timeoutMs: 75` while the socket stayed open.
- Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the real local Gateway/WebSocket/Control UI client run, with token, isolated home path, and generated device identity redacted:

```json
{
```

Copied live output from the no-response timeout harness, with token and generated device identity redacted:

```json

        "scenario"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The real Gateway browser client completed `hello-ok` with protocol 4 as operator, received a device token, successfully read live Gateway `health`, emitted successful `connect` and `health` timings, and the temporary Gateway was stopped afterward. The no-response harness then kept an actual WebSocket open, deliberately did not answer `proof.neverResponds`, and the actual `GatewayBrowserClient` rejected with `GatewayRequestTimeoutError` after the 75ms client timeout while emitting a single `CLIENT_TIMEOUT` timing and remaining connected before stop.
- What was not tested: No deploy, canary, Testbox, release validation, Golden Run success, end-to-end product flow, or MVP score movement. The no-response proof uses an actual local WebSocket server to create the unanswered-request condition; it is not a deployed Gateway canary or release proof.

Historical pre-rebase proof retained for traceability: The earlier real setup proof was captured before the branch was rebased, at SHA e906ef282a95277a4355efd42c2d8ea0ac441d92 on port 18976. It is retained only as historical traceability and should not be treated as the current PR head proof. Historical observed result: the browser client completed `hello-ok` with protocol 3 as operator, received a device token, successfully read live Gateway `health`, emitted successful `connect` and `health` timings, and the temporary Gateway was stopped afterward.

Calibration notes: A query-side CLI attempt without the isolated `HOME` read the operator's real local config and failed on unrelated stale model IDs; rerunning the same health check with isolated local state succeeded. Raw Node socket, missing Origin, and missing WebCrypto identity attempts were rejected during earlier calibration, so the live proof used a browser-shaped client path.

Release evidence and claim ceiling
- This real behavior proof is bounded to a local real OpenClaw Gateway/WebSocket/Control UI client path plus a local actual-WebSocket no-response timeout harness at the current PR head SHA.
- It does not prove deploy, canary, Testbox, release validation, Golden Run success, end-to-end product flow, or MVP score movement.
- No changelog entry is included yet because this branch was intentionally kept to the bounded two-file Card 6 patch.

target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): add retry mechanism for Windows Docker bind mount sync delays
target.body:
Hey! 👋

This PR hardens the memory-lancedb plugin initialization path for transient LanceDB table metadata errors seen with Docker bind-mounted data directories.

## The Problem

When a LanceDB data directory is backed by a host bind mount, table metadata can be briefly unavailable after table creation. The previous one-shot initialization path could fail during startup, and the first retry version still had two gaps:

- a table created before `__schema__` cleanup could be reopened and used before cleanup was retried
- the bind-mount guidance was gated on `process.platform === "win32"`, which misses Linux containers running on Docker Desktop

## The Solution

The retry loop now keeps the table local until cleanup succeeds, retries cleanup when a table is reopened, and surfaces generic Docker bind-mount guidance after retry exhaustion.

## What Changed

- Updated `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` to only cache the table after `__schema__` cleanup succeeds.
- Added Docker bind-mount guidance on retry exhaustion regardless of host/runtime platform.
- Added focused coverage in `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts` for transient create-cleanup failure and exhausted retry diagnostics.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** memory-lancedb can initialize a LanceDB-backed memory table, store a memory, and recall it after this initialization retry hardening.

**Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw plugin registration path using the actual memory-lancedb plugin, actual `@lancedb/lancedb` storage in a temporary database, and a local OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```console
$ NODE_OPTIONS=--no-deprecation node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-memory-lancedb-real-smoke.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:** copied live console output from the real smoke run:

```console
OpenClaw memory-lancedb real setup smoke
dbPath: <temp>/lancedb
registered tools: memory_forget, memory_recall, memory_store
embedding requests: 2
store action: created
stored id format: uuid
recall count: 1
recalled category: preference
recalled text: I prefer deterministic OpenClaw memory proof runs.
startup log: info:memory-lancedb: plugin registered (db: <temp>/lancedb, lazy init)
```

**Observed result after fix:** `memory_store` created a UUID-backed memory in the LanceDB table, and `memory_recall` returned one recalled preference from that same temporary LanceDB database.

**What was not tested:** I did not run a Windows Docker Desktop bind-mount reproduction locally. The real smoke covers the actual plugin + LanceDB store/recall path; the focused regression tests cover transient cleanup retry and the exhausted-retry Docker bind-mount diagnostic.

## Testing

```console
$ pnpm test extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  46 passed (46)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 12.34s
```

```console
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```console
$ node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

```console
$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Fixes #58139

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(openai): prefix instruction-aware embedding queries
target.body:
## Summary

AI-assisted PR.

- Problem: OpenAI-compatible embedding providers passed raw query text to instruction-aware embedding models such as Qwen3, Nomic, and mxbai.
- Why it matters: Raw queries produced lower-quality retrieval for instruction-aware embedding models because query embeddings did not match the model's expected retrieval-query format.
- What changed: OpenAI embeddings now apply model-specific query instruction prefixes for query embeddings, while document batch embeddings remain unchanged.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No embedding defaults, credentials handling, base URL behavior, document embedding inputs, or non-OpenAI providers were changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenAI-compatible embedding providers now apply model-specific query instruction prefixes for instruction-aware Qwen3, Nomic, and Mixedbread embedding model families on query embeddings, while document/batch embeddings remain raw.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw development checkout at PR head `65a5ce21910de9a43be7bed4ff1d22ab3f757b4b`, using configured model `Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B` against an OpenAI-compatible vLLM endpoint on coedo. A local redacting/proof proxy only rewrote the model id for backend compatibility and captured the request bodies emitted by the providers.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `tmp/pr-79702-current-head-proof.mts` from the PR worktree after the current-head rebase. The script exercised both touched provider paths: bundled OpenAI query/document embeddings and generic `openai-compatible` query/document embeddings.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal/request-capture output from the current-head proof run:

```text
Current head: 65a5ce21910de9a43be7bed4ff1d22ab3f757b4b
Generated at: 2026-06-02T19:18:56.751Z
Configured model: Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B
bundled OpenAI query vector dimensions: 2560
bundled OpenAI document vector dimensions: 2560
generic openai-compatible query vector dimensions: 2560
generic openai-compatible document vector dimensions: 2560
```

Captured redacted request bodies:

```json
[
  {
    "providerPath": "bundled-openai",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "Instruct: Given a user query, retrieve relevant memory notes and documents\nQuery:where is the local OpenClaw fork for PR 79702?"
      ]
    }
  },
  {
    "providerPath": "bundled-openai",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "PR 79702 local fork is under ~/.openclaw/tmp/openclaw-pr/openclaw."
      ]
    }
  },
  {
    "providerPath": "generic-openai-compatible",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "Instruct: Given a user query, retrieve relevant memory notes and documents\nQuery:where is the local OpenClaw fork for PR 79702?"
      ]
    }
  },
  {
    "providerPath": "generic-openai-compatible",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "PR 79702 local fork is under ~/.openclaw/tmp/openclaw-pr/openclaw."
      ]
    }
  }
]
```

**Observed result after fix:** Both touched provider paths returned 2560-dimensional vectors from live calls. Query request bodies were prefixed with `Instruct: Given a user query, retrieve relevant memory notes and documents\nQuery:...`; document request bodies stayed raw for the same configured model.

**What was not tested:** Every OpenAI-compatible embedding server and additional instruction-aware model families beyond the covered Qwen3, Nomic, and Mixedbread matchers were not tested.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The OpenAI embedding adapter did not mirror the instruction-aware query-prefix handling already used by the Ollama adapter.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Unit tests did not verify provider-specific query payloads for instruction-aware models.
- Contributing context (if known): Some OpenAI-compatible servers expose embedding models that expect different query and document input formatting even when they use an OpenAI-style `/embeddings` API.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
 - [x] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [ ] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Query embeddings for Qwen3, Nomic, and mxbai models include the expected query instruction prefix; document batch embeddings remain raw.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior is local to the OpenAI embedding adapter request payload construction.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

OpenAI-compatible embedding providers now format query embeddings with instruction prefixes for known instruction-aware embedding models. Document embeddings and default OpenAI embedding models are unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[memory search query] -> [raw query embedding input] -> [lower-quality retrieval for instruction-aware models]

After:
[memory search query] -> [model-specific query instruction prefix] -> [embedding input] -> [improved retrieval in tested setup]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw development checkout
- Model/provider: qwen3-embedding-4b through an OpenAI-compatible vLLM endpoint
- Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw memorySearch
- Relevant config (redacted): OpenAI-compatible embedding provider pointed at a local vLLM endpoint; endpoint details redacted.

### Steps

1. Configure OpenClaw memory search to use `qwen3-embedding-4b` via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
2. Run the same 24-query live retrieval evaluation before and after the adapter change.
3. Compare top-3 recall and observed query latency in that setup.

### Expected

- Query embeddings for known instruction-aware models include the model-specific query prefix.
- Document embeddings are not query-prefixed.

### Actual

- Query embeddings for Qwen3, Nomic, and mxbai model IDs are prefixed as expected.
- Document embedding payloads remain raw.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Verification run:

- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH" pnpm test:extension openai` - 22 files, 241 tests passed
- `PATH="/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH" pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts extensions/openai/embedding-provider.test.ts` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Live memorySearch evaluation before and after the query-prefix change; unit tests for prefixed query embeddings and raw document embeddings.
- Edge cases checked: Empty input still returns an empty result; unknown models continue using raw queries; blank configured models still fall back to the default embedding model.
- What you did **not** verify: Every OpenAI-compatible embedding server or additional instruction-aware model family beyond the covered prefixes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A provider could expose a model ID with one of these prefixes but expect raw query input.
 - Mitigation: Prefix matching is limited to known instruction-aware model families and only applies to query embeddings; document embeddings remain unchanged.

- Real environment tested: macOS 26.4.1 arm64 with Node 24.14.0 against a local OpenClaw checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rg -n POSIX systems|gateway user uid|root-owned docs/gateway/secrets.md docs/cli/secrets.md docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md

  docs/cli/secrets.md:151: On POSIX systems, provider paths normally must be owned by the gateway user uid; root-owned system-package binaries such as /usr/bin/op or /usr/bin/sops can fail this check.
  docs/gateway/secrets.md:170: On POSIX systems, the path must normally be owned by the gateway user uid.
  docs/gateway/secrets.md:179: On POSIX systems, the command path must normally be owned by the gateway user uid, so root-owned package-manager binaries such as /usr/bin/op or /usr/bin/sops can require allowInsecurePath: true.
  docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md:892: On POSIX systems, provider paths normally must be owned by the gateway user uid, so root-owned system-package binaries can also fail verification.

target.title: security fix(gateway): defend pre-auth device-signature verify against CPU DoS
target.body:
Closes #77979

## Summary

- Pre-auth WS handshakes that include a `device` block ran `crypto.createPublicKey` plus a v3-then-v2 `crypto.verify` per request, gated only by frame-size limits. An unauthenticated remote attacker could pin a single Gateway core: PoC against a real running gateway hits **~510 forged handshakes/s/IP**, degrading legit handshake p50 by **48.7×** (1.16 ms → 56.6 ms) and p99 by **94.7×**.
- Fix is layered: schema cap on `device.publicKey` (1024) and `device.signature` (256); shape pre-check on `verifyDeviceSignature` / `deriveDeviceIdFromPublicKey` / `normalizeDevicePublicKeyBase64Url` rejecting inputs that cannot decode to a 32-byte raw key or 64-byte signature; new `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` bucket gating the verify per IP. The browser-origin rate limiter is always constructed (`server.impl.ts:424`), so the gate fires for browser-origin clients even when no `gateway.auth.rateLimit` is configured. For non-browser-origin clients, the gate fires when the operator configures a rate limiter (the typical production case).
- Loopback exemption is preserved so legit local CLI sessions are never throttled; the gate fires through the non-loopback-exempt limiter for remote and browser-origin clients, which is the production threat path.

## Reproduction

The PoC (`scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs`) opens N concurrent WS connections, each completing the connect-challenge handshake with a real Ed25519 PEM public key and a random 64-byte signature. The gateway reaches the verify path (server-issued nonce echoed back, `device.id` matches `sha256(rawPublicKey)`) and runs `createPublicKey` + v3 `verify` + v2 `verify` per request. A separate measurement client probes time-to-first-event in parallel.

Against a vulnerable gateway:

```
pnpm gateway:watch                                    # in another terminal

node scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs \
     --gateway ws://127.0.0.1:18789 \
     --attackers 32 \
     --legit-iters 30 \
     --duration-ms 3000
```

Observed:

```
baseline:     n=30 p50=1.16ms  p90=1.80ms  p99=2.04ms
under-attack: n=30 p50=56.58ms p90=64.24ms p99=193.36ms
attacker totals: connections=1531  rate=510/s   (one IP, one node process)
legit handshake p50 ratio (under-attack / baseline): 48.69x
legit handshake p99 ratio: 94.73x
```

To validate the fix end-to-end without exposing a dev gateway to the LAN, the in-process integration test runs the same forged-handshake sequence against a real `withGatewayServer` instance configured with `rateLimit.maxAttempts: 1, exemptLoopback: true`:

```
pnpm test src/gateway/server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts
```

The test asserts:
1. The first N forged signatures fail with `error.details.reason === "device-signature"` (the verify ran).
2. The (N+1)th attempt fails with `error.details.reason === "device-signature-rate-limited"` and `retryAfterMs > 0` — the gate short-circuited before any crypto.

Reverting the `recordFailure` line in the rate-limit gate makes both tests fail with `expected 'device-signature' to be 'device-signature-rate-limited'`.

## Test plan

- [x] `src/gateway/server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts` — new in-process WS integration test (2 cases). Both fail when the fix is disabled.
- [x] `src/infra/device-identity.test.ts` — new shape pre-check tests: oversized PEM, oversized non-PEM, signature-shaped input rejected as public key, public-key-shaped input rejected as signature, all without invoking crypto.
- [x] `src/gateway/protocol/connect-device-bounds.test.ts` — new schema-cap tests: `validateConnectParams` rejects oversized `device.publicKey` and `device.signature`; accepts them at the documented bounds.
- [x] Existing `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts` — 27/27 pass, no regression.
- [x] Live PoC reproduction confirms the 50× p50 / 95× p99 degradation pre-fix.

## Verification

- Targeted `pnpm test` of the four affected files: 41/41 pass.
- Build: dist contains the new rate-limit gate (`grep AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` on the bundle).
- Type errors observed during local `pnpm tsgo` are pre-existing and unrelated (`pi-embedded-runner.*` AgentMessage casts and missing `web-tree-sitter` dep).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: pre-auth `device` blocks ran `crypto.createPublicKey` plus a v3-then-v2 `crypto.verify` per request with no per-IP cap. An unauthenticated attacker could pin a Gateway core (~510 forged handshakes/s/IP, 48.7× p50 / 94.7× p99 degradation against legitimate handshakes per the network-level PoC numbers above). The fix layers three defenses: ajv schema caps reject oversized `device.publicKey` and `device.signature` before any handler runs; `isPlausibleDevicePublicKeyInput` / `isPlausibleDeviceSignatureInput` shape pre-checks reject inputs that cannot decode to a 32-byte raw key or 64-byte signature without invoking crypto; and `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` gates the verify per IP, so once the bucket is exhausted no `createPublicKey` or `verify` runs.
- **Real environment tested**: local Node v22.22.2 runtime against the real patched modules from this branch (`security/preauth-signature-rate-limit` HEAD `6233fc0e60`). No vitest, no mocks. The reproducer imports `validateConnectParams` from the patched `protocol/index.ts`, the cap constants and `isPlausibleDevice*` helpers from `infra/device-identity.ts`, and `createAuthRateLimiter` from `gateway/auth-rate-limit.ts`, then exercises each layer against representative attacker payloads.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: a node runtime tsx reproducer at https://gist.github.com/fede-kamel/974f456b4269b7242e1c91b9dc46c178 runs three layers — schema caps against oversized `device.publicKey`/`device.signature` (including a 60 KB representative attacker payload), shape pre-checks against malformed inputs that decode to wrong-length raw bytes, and the `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE` bucket fired against a single attacker IP. Run as `node --import tsx <local-copy>.mts` from a worktree on this branch.
- **Evidence after fix**: live terminal output captured from the node runtime against the patched modules:

```
Pre-auth device-signature CPU-DoS proof (PR 77492)
Imports real patched validateConnectParams + isPlausibleDevice* + AuthRateLimiter
from security/preauth-signature-rate-limit worktree.

MAX_DEVICE_PUBLIC_KEY_INPUT_CHARS = 1024
MAX_DEVICE_SIGNATURE_INPUT_CHARS  = 256
ED25519_RAW_PUBLIC_KEY_BYTES      = 32
ED25519_SIGNATURE_BYTES           = 64

=== Layer 1: schema-level rejection of oversized device.publicKey / device.signature ===

case                                                          | result | took
--------------------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: device.publicKey real PEM (113 chars)                  | PASS   | 4.494ms
reject: device.publicKey at 1025 chars (cap+1)                | REJECT | 0.515ms
reject: device.publicKey at 60KB (representative attacker payload)| REJECT | 4.246ms
reject: device.signature at 257 chars (cap+1)                 | REJECT | 0.077ms

=== Layer 2: shape pre-check on device-identity helpers (no crypto) ===

case                                                          | result | took
--------------------------------------------------------------|--------|------
valid: real raw 32-byte base64url public key                  | PASS   | 0.486ms
valid: real PEM public key                                    | PASS   | 0.039ms
reject: random 100-byte base64                                | REJECT | 0.629ms
reject: oversized publicKey (cap+1)                           | REJECT | 0.053ms
reject: empty publicKey                                       | REJECT | 0.048ms
valid: real 64-byte base64url signature                       | PASS   | 0.101ms
reject: random 32-byte signature input                        | REJECT | 0.118ms
reject: oversized signature (cap+1)                           | REJECT | 0.035ms
reject: 60KB signature                                        | REJECT | 0.044ms

=== Layer 3: rate-limit gate AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_SCOPE_DEVICE_SIGNATURE ===

attempt | rateLimited | remaining/retryAfterMs | crypto entered?
--------|-------------|------------------------|------------------
    1   | false       | remaining=3            | would enter
    2   | false       | remaining=2            | would enter
    3   | false       | remaining=1            | would enter
    4   | true        | retryAfterMs=60000     | skipped
    5   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    6   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    7   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped
    8   | true        | retryAfterMs=59999     | skipped

=== Layer 3b: cost comparison — skipped vs entered ===

crypto entered 5000 times: 214.3ms (avg 0.043ms/op)
gate-blocked  5000 times: 62.6ms (avg 0.013ms/op)
amplification factor under attack: 3.4x
```

- **Observed result after fix**: every legitimate input validated `PASS` and every cap+1 / 60 KB / wrong-length / wrong-shape attacker input validated `REJECT` across both schema (Layer 1) and shape pre-check (Layer 2). The rate-limit gate (Layer 3) blocked attempts 4 through 8 from a single attacker IP after the configured `maxAttempts: 3`, never reaching `createPublicKey`/`verify`. Layer 3b measured the per-op cost of the full Ed25519 verify path (0.043 ms/op) versus the gate-blocked path (0.013 ms/op) — a 3.4× per-op CPU saving on every attempt past the threshold, which compounds against the 510/s/IP attacker rate observed in the network-level PoC.
- **What was not tested**: the WS upgrade and connect-challenge wire protocol are not driven by this reproducer; that integration shape is covered by `server.preauth-signature-rate-limit.test.ts` (in-process WS server) and by the live `scripts/poc-preauth-flood-ws.mjs` PoC against a running `pnpm gateway:watch`. Concurrent multi-IP attacker behavior was not measured in this reproducer; the gate is per-IP and isolation matches the existing device-token bucket.

## Notes for reviewer

- This branch is also flagged `triage: dirty-candidate` because the diff touches schema, device-identity helpers, and the WS message handler. The three layers are intentionally tightly coupled — the schema cap is a cheap fast-reject, the shape pre-check covers in-spec but malformed inputs that pass the schema, and the rate-limit gate covers attackers presenting in-shape Ed25519 keypairs (their own). Splitting the layers would either leave one layer behind a partial defense or duplicate the fix surface across PRs. Happy to split if reviewers prefer one layer per PR.
- The `recordFailure` is intentionally pessimistic before crypto runs — an attacker with their own keypair producing valid self-signatures should still consume the bucket. `reset` only fires after `resolveConnectAuthDecision` confirms the full handshake is authorized.

target.title: Add prototype pollution guard to resolveConfigPath
target.body:
## Summary

`resolveConfigPath` in `src/shared/config-eval.ts` traverses config objects by dot-separated path strings (e.g. `"foo.bar.baz"`), but does not check whether any path segment is a dangerous prototype key (`__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor`). This means a crafted path string could be used to access or leak prototype chain properties.

Other path-traversal functions in the codebase already guard against this:
- `parseConfigPath` in `src/config/config-paths.ts`
- `merge-patch.ts`, `runtime-overrides.ts`, `includes.ts`, and others

All of these use the shared `isBlockedObjectKey` utility from `src/infra/prototype-keys.ts`.

## Changes

- Import `isBlockedObjectKey` from `../infra/prototype-keys.js`
- Add a guard inside the `resolveConfigPath` loop that returns `undefined` when a blocked key is encountered
- Matches the existing security pattern used across the codebase

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing tests for `resolveConfigPath` and `isConfigPathTruthyWithDefaults` continue to pass
- [ ] Paths containing `__proto__`, `prototype`, or `constructor` segments now return `undefined`
- [ ] Normal dot-separated paths still resolve correctly

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `resolveConfigPath` now blocks prototype-key path segments before object property access while preserving normal dot-path resolution.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-59694`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
rg -n "isBlockedObjectKey|blocks prototype keys" src/shared/config-eval.ts src/shared/config-eval.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
src/shared/config-eval.test.ts:62:  it("blocks prototype keys while resolving config paths", () => {
src/shared/config-eval.ts:4:import { isBlockedObjectKey } from "../infra/prototype-keys.js";
src/shared/config-eval.ts:31:    if (isBlockedObjectKey(part)) {
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched resolver has a real guard before object access and the regression coverage keeps `safe.enabled` resolving while `__proto__`, `constructor.name`, and `prototype.polluted` return `undefined`.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: fix(agents): emit reasoning stream events to gateway clients without channel callback (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Gateway-connected clients (webchat, custom WebSocket clients) never receive live `agent stream=thinking` events during agent runs, even when the user has set `reasoningLevel: "stream"`. Thinking only surfaces post-run via `chat.history`.
- **Why it matters:** Anyone building a non-channel WS client (webchat, mobile apps over the gateway) can't render reasoning tokens live. The plumbing exists end-to-end on both sides — gateway broadcasts `agent stream=thinking`, `chat.history` returns thinking blocks — but a single guard short-circuits emission for the gateway path.
- **What changed:** Two edits in `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts`. (1) Decouple `streamReasoning` from `onReasoningStream`. Keep `canShowReasoning` so the visibility gate still applies. (2) Make `params.onReasoningStream(...)` invocation null-safe with `?.()` so existing channel callers (Telegram, Mattermost) keep working.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No protocol change. No UI rendering changes. Only the chat.send → auto-reply path was tested live — entry points like cron and ACP go through `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts` and were not exercised.

> AI-assisted: yes (Claude Code). Live behavior verified by the author on a real WS client.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- Addresses #42261, #51505, #48995 — all describe the same `streamReasoning` callback-requirement bug fixed here. (Already closed without a fix landing; flagging as cross-references.)
- Partially addresses #5086 — this PR enables gateway emission of `stream: "thinking"` events. The webchat UI rendering side is a follow-up.
- Related #47613 — open PR with the same root cause. This PR keeps `canShowReasoning` (codex review concern on #47613) and adds the no-callback regression test the review asked for.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** With `reasoningLevel: "stream"` set, gateway WS clients receive zero `agent stream=thinking` events during a chat.send-originated agent run.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway (this branch installed at `~/.nvm/.../node_modules/openclaw/`), Linux 6.17 / Node 24.13. WS client: a custom Android client that decodes `agent stream=thinking` events and renders matching `type=thinking` content blocks. Inference: GitHub Copilot, GPT 5.4.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `openclaw config set agents.defaults.reasoningDefault stream`
  2. Restart gateway
  3. Open the WS client, send a prompt that elicits reasoning
- **Evidence after fix:** Screen recording attached below — thinking content streams live during the turn rather than only appearing after `chat.history` reload.


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d206c652-7d84-43e0-9c3c-744b20d9f0d9



- **Observed result after fix:** Live `agent` events with `stream: "thinking"` and `data: { text, delta }` arrive on the WS during the turn.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Providers other than GitHub Copilot / GPT 5.4.
  - Entry points other than chat.send → auto-reply (cron, ACP, etc.). Those go through `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts` and I did not exercise them; behavior there may or may not be equivalent.
  - Channel paths (Telegram preview, Mattermost draft) — only covered by the existing test suite, not live.
  - Control UI rendering of the new event stream (separate follow-up).
  - Multi-agent / subagent runs.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `streamReasoning` in `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts:139` required **both** `reasoningMode === "stream"` AND `typeof params.onReasoningStream === "function"` to flip true. `chat.send` (`src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`) doesn't supply that callback in `replyOptions`, so the flag stayed false for chat.send-originated runs and `emitReasoningStream` short-circuited before the global `emitAgentEvent({ stream: "thinking", ... })` could fire.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No no-callback test coverage for `streamReasoning`. Existing reasoning-stream tests at `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.subscribeembeddedpisession.test.ts:184,718,754` all instantiate `onReasoningStream`.
- **Contributing context:** The callback dependency made sense when reasoning streaming was Telegram-preview-only. Adding `emitAgentEvent({ stream: "thinking" })` for gateway clients made the callback no longer the only sink, but the guard wasn't loosened.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:**
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.subscribeembeddedpisession.test.ts` — new `describe` block with 2 tests.
- **Scenario the test should lock in:**
  1. `reasoningMode: "stream"` with no `onReasoningStream` callback → `agent stream=thinking` events fire on the global event bus.
  2. `reasoningMode: "stream"` with `thinkingLevel: "off"` → no thinking events (visibility gate honored — `canShowReasoning` preserved).
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Pinning the no-callback path catches any refactor that re-introduces a callback dependency. The visibility-gate test pins the `canShowReasoning` invariant separately so a naive simplification can't broaden reasoning exposure.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None — existing tests at lines 184, 718, 754 all supply `onReasoningStream`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When `reasoningLevel: "stream"` is set (via `agents.defaults.reasoningDefault: "stream"` or `/reasoning stream`):

- WS clients subscribed to `agent` events now receive `stream: "thinking"` events with `data: { text, delta }` during agent turns.
- No behavior change for `reasoningMode: "off"` or `"on"`, or for any session where `thinkingLevel: "off"` is set.
- No behavior change for channel paths that already supply `onReasoningStream`.

## Diagram

`N/A`

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — the `agent stream=thinking` broadcast already exists on the same code path; this PR stops short-circuiting before it fires. Visibility gating (`canShowReasoning` from `thinkingLevel !== "off"`) preserved.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17
- Runtime: Node 24.13.1, gateway as systemd user service
- Model/provider: GitHub Copilot, GPT 5.4
- Integration/channel: Custom WS client over `chat.send` / `chat.history`
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.reasoningDefault: "stream"`

### Steps

1. Build & install this branch into the gateway location.
2. `openclaw config set agents.defaults.reasoningDefault stream`
3. Restart the gateway.
4. Connect a WS client that handles `agent stream=thinking` events.
5. Send a prompt that triggers reasoning.

### Expected

`agent` events with `stream: "thinking"`, `data: { text, delta }` arrive during the run. `text` is cumulative; `delta` is the new text since the last event.

### Actual

Matches expected. See attached recording.

## Evidence

- [x] New regression tests pass
- [x] Screen recording (attached above)

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - Live thinking streams on a custom WS client with `reasoningLevel: "stream"` and GitHub Copilot / GPT 5.4 — recording attached.
  - The two new regression tests pass; the modified test file is 39/39.
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Other providers, other reasoning-capable models.
  - Entry points other than chat.send → auto-reply (cron, ACP, etc.).
  - Channel paths (Telegram / Mattermost) live — only via existing tests.
  - Control UI rendering of the new stream.
  - Multi-agent / subagent runs.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A user with `reasoningMode: "stream"` and `thinkingLevel: "off"` would, with the original PR #47613 patch, start seeing live reasoning broadcast — broader than intended.
  - **Mitigation:** This PR keeps `canShowReasoning`. Test 2 in the new suite pins this invariant.
- **Risk:** A future refactor re-introduces the callback dependency, silently regressing chat.send / WS clients.
  - **Mitigation:** Test 1 asserts `agent stream=thinking` events fire when `onReasoningStream` is absent, against the global event bus.

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: fix(whatsapp): preserve auth on terminal disconnects
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve WhatsApp Web credentials on passive terminal disconnects instead of deleting auth from the monitor.
- Track same-process logged-out terminal state inside the WhatsApp plugin so explicit login/QR relink can clear stale creds deliberately.
- Clear that marker only after verified recovery paths: monitor reconnect, successful CLI login, successful QR login, or successful explicit logout cleanup.
- Keep logged-out login retry bounded to one cleanup/replacement and share the socket replacement path with the existing 408/515 restart handling.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/logout.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel.logout.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`
- `git diff --check origin/main`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp passive monitor terminal disconnects no longer remove saved credentials, while explicit login/relink/logout flows still clean stale auth when they own the action.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw WhatsApp unit/e2e harness in the branch worktree.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/logout.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/channel.logout.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: The focused run passed 1 e2e file with 26 tests and 6 WhatsApp extension files with 62 tests.

Observed result after fix: Passive logged-out monitor paths preserve auth and mark relink state; QR/CLI relink clears stale credentials deliberately; successful relink clears the marker so fresh credentials are not deleted later.

What was not tested: No live WhatsApp/Baileys QR scan, real-device logout, or conflict-session scenario was run.

target.title: fix(doctor): suppress false groupAllowFrom warning when per-account allowlists cover all accounts
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw doctor` warns that inbound group messages will be dropped when the top-level `groupAllowFrom` is empty, even when every account has its own populated `groupAllowFrom`. At runtime, per-account allowlists handle routing correctly.
- **Why now:** Users with per-account allowlists see a misleading warning that contradicts actual behavior.
- **Outcome:** Only check the top-level channel config if it has no accounts. When accounts exist, the top-level config is a fallback parent, not a real account.

## Linked context

Closes #92684

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor` falsely warns about empty top-level `groupAllowFrom` when every account already provides its own populated allowlist.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 10, Node.js v22.22.3, OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (48596b9), local checkout at `upstream/main` with the patch applied.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 
  1. Ran `openclaw doctor` to verify no false warnings
  2. Ran regression tests via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor/shared/empty-allowlist-scan.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from OpenClaw CLI:
  ```
  $ openclaw doctor
  
  ◇  Security ─────────────────────────────────╮
  │                                            │
  │  - No channel security warnings detected.  │
  │                                            │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  ```
  No false groupAllowFrom warnings emitted.
  
  Regression test output:
  ```
  ✓ doctor empty allowlist policy scan > does not warn on top-level groupAllowFrom when every account has its own allowlist (regression #92684)
  Tests 4 passed (4)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** No warnings are emitted when every account has its own populated `groupAllowFrom`.
- **Before evidence:** Same test with the fix reverted shows:
  ```
  ✗ does not warn on top-level groupAllowFrom when every account has its own allowlist (regression #92684)
    AssertionError: expected [ Array(1) ] to equal []
  ```
- **What was not tested:** Live `openclaw doctor` with real Telegram channel configuration (requires Telegram bot setup).

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor/shared/empty-allowlist-scan.test.ts` — 4/4 passed

## Risk checklist

- **userVisible:** Yes (false warning suppressed)
- **configEnvMigration:** No
- **securityAuthNetwork:** No
- **Mitigation:** Only suppresses warning when every account has its own allowlist; top-level-only configs still warn.

target.title: fix(agents): resolve bound agent workspace for unscoped inbound session keys (fixes #92903)
target.body:
Fixes #92903

## Summary

Multi-agent inbound turns could load the wrong workspace: a channel message routed to a bound, non-default agent injected its system-prompt **Project Context** (`SOUL.md`/`AGENTS.md`/`USER.md`/`IDENTITY.md`) from the **default** workspace instead of the bound agent's own workspace.

**Root cause:** `buildChannelInboundEventContext` built the inbound context from the resolved route but dropped `route.agentId`, so `ctx.AgentId` was never populated. Downstream agent resolution (`resolveSessionAgentId` in `initSessionState`) then depended solely on the session key. When the resolved session key did not encode an agent id (e.g. an unscoped binding session key), the run fell back to the **default** agent — and its workspace.

**Fix:**
- Carry the routed bound agent into the inbound context (`AgentId`) from `route.agentId`
- Have `initSessionState` use `AgentId` as a fallback when resolving the session agent
- Precedence: explicit agentId → session-key agent → bound route agent → default

## Changes

- `src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts`: Add `AgentId: params.route.agentId` to the context built by `buildChannelInboundEventContext`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`: Pass `sessionCtxForState.AgentId` as fallback in `initSessionState` so the bound agent is resolved correctly for unscoped session keys

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Multi-agent inbound workspace resolution: inbound channel turns for a bound non-default agent resolve the agent's own workspace/bootstrap even when the resolved session key is unscoped, instead of defaulting to the default agent's workspace.

**Real environment tested:**
Node.js v24.16.0, Linux 6.18.33.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2, OpenClaw commit f1b8827d (upstream/main).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
cd /root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-repo && git diff upstream/main
```

**Evidence after fix:**
```terminal output
diff --git a/src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts b/src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts
index a2b1a645..4d423af3 100644
--- a/src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts
+++ b/src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts
@@ -272,6 +272,7 @@ export async function initSessionState(params: {
   const agentId = resolveSessionAgentId({
     sessionKey: sessionCtxForState.SessionKey,
     config: cfg,
+    agentId: sessionCtxForState.AgentId,
   });
   const groupResolution = resolveGroupSessionKey(sessionCtxForState) ?? undefined;
diff --git a/src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts b/src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts
index 0cd442ca..e3889f9a 100644
--- a/src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts
+++ b/src/channels/inbound-event/context.ts
@@ -510,6 +510,7 @@ export function buildChannelInboundEventContext(
     OriginatingChannel: params.channel,
     OriginatingTo: params.reply.originatingTo ?? params.reply.to,
     ThreadParentId: params.reply.threadParentId ?? params.conversation.parentId,
+    AgentId: params.route.agentId,
     ...params.extra,
   };
```

**Observed result after fix:**
The bound agent's workspace bootstrap files are the ones resolved for the run when `AgentId` is populated from the route context. Standard agent-scoped session keys are unaffected.

**What was not tested:**
No live multi-agent gateway deployment with an unscoped binding session key. The fix is verified at the code-path level: `AgentId` is now propagated through the inbound context and used as a fallback in agent resolution. The existing `dispatch-from-config.ts` and `get-reply.ts` already consume `ctx.AgentId` correctly.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Documentation update

Adds `openclaw sessions compact <key>` backed by the existing gateway RPC, rejects CLI `/compact` messages with exit 1, documents the command/RPC, and adds an active-run guard for `sessions.compact` maxLines truncation.

PR surface: Source +256, Tests +312, Docs +51. Total +619 across 9 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main exposes the `sessions.compact` RPC but not a `sessions compact` CLI command, and the current CLI `/compact` path only blocks embedded timeout fallback rather than failing before dispatch; the linked issue also gives concrete command-level reproduction.
commands.review_followup: - No automated repair is indicated; maintainer review should decide whether to accept the compatibility-visible exit behavior and session-state guard, then land this or choose a canonical competing PR.

target.title: feat(cli): add `openclaw sessions compact` and fail loudly on CLI `/compact` (fixes #90640)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90640.

`sessions.compact` was reachable only as an internal Gateway RPC: there was no
CLI command and no docs, and `openclaw agent --message '/compact'` silently
no-opped with exit 0 (the slash-command handler rejects CLI-originated senders,
so the message fell through to an ordinary agent turn that compacted nothing).

This PR closes both gaps:

- Adds `openclaw sessions compact <key>` as a first-class command wrapping the
  existing `sessions.compact` RPC. It exits non-zero whenever the gateway
  reports a failed compaction — both a transport error and an `ok:false`
  payload — so automation never mistakes a silent no-op for success.
- Makes `openclaw agent --message '/compact'` fail loudly: it prints a redirect
  to `openclaw sessions compact <key>` and exits 1 instead of silently exiting
  0. The shared chat-side `/compact` handler is left untouched, so chat
  behaviour and message delivery are unchanged.
- Inherits the parent `sessions` options for `compact`, matching its sibling
  subcommands (`list`/`cleanup`/`info`/…). Previously a parent-level
  `--agent`/`--json` was silently dropped, so `openclaw sessions --agent work
  compact <key>` compacted the **default** agent's session instead of the
  `work` agent's — a wrong-target session-state mutation. The compact-level
  option still takes precedence over the inherited parent option.
- Rejects unsupported inherited parent options for `compact` (`--store`,
  `--all-agents`, `--active`, `--limit`): the gateway resolves the target store
  from `<key>`/`--agent`, so honoring a parent `--store` is impossible and
  silently dropping it could mutate a different store than the user named. These
  are now rejected with a clear error and a non-zero exit instead of ignored.
- Reports an asynchronously *started* Codex compaction (`thread/compact/start`,
  `ok:true` / `compacted:false` / `pending:true`) as pending instead of
  collapsing it into a misleading "No compaction needed" message.
- Guards the `--max-lines` truncate path against active runs. The non-maxLines
  (LLM) compact branch already interrupts an active session run before
  compacting, but the maxLines branch archived and overwrote the transcript in
  place without that guard. Exposing `--max-lines` as a documented CLI flag would
  otherwise make the active-run data-loss mode tracked by #72765 easy to hit, so
  the maxLines branch now runs the same `interruptSessionRunIfActive` guard
  **before** reading the tail, archiving, and truncating — parity with the LLM
  compact path.
- Strictly validates `--max-lines` and `--timeout` (positive integers only).
- Documents the command and the `sessions.compact` RPC in docs/cli/sessions.md.

## Why this is safe

Two scopes change, both narrow:

- **CLI surface** (command registration + `--message` dispatch). This does
  **not** modify the shared `commands-compact.ts` handler that powers chat
  `/compact`, so chat behaviour and message delivery are unchanged — only the
  CLI path that was already broken changes.
- **Gateway `sessions.compact` maxLines branch** gains the active-run interrupt
  guard. This reuses the existing `interruptSessionRunIfActive` helper — the
  exact call the non-maxLines (LLM) compact branch already makes — and runs it in
  the maxLines truncate branch before it reads the tail, archives, and overwrites
  the transcript. It does not redesign compact/session ownership and is scoped to
  active-run parity for the entry point this PR newly exposes; it can supersede
  the narrow guard portion of #72765 for that path.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Five behaviors of the latest head are
  exercised. (A) `openclaw sessions compact <key>` drives a real compaction
  end-to-end through the new CLI command body (not just the raw RPC). (B)
  `openclaw agent --message '/compact'` fails loudly with a non-zero exit
  instead of silently exiting 0. (C) a Codex app-server `thread/compact/start`
  result (`ok:true` / `compacted:false` / `pending:true`) is rendered as a
  *started* compaction, not collapsed into "No compaction needed". (D)
  unsupported inherited parent `sessions` options for `compact`
  (`--store`/`--all-agents`/`--active`/`--limit`) are rejected with a clear
  error and a non-zero exit instead of being silently dropped. (E) the maxLines
  truncate path runs the same active-run interrupt guard as the LLM compact
  branch before archiving and truncating, so exposing `--max-lines` from the CLI
  does not make the #72765 data-loss mode easier to hit.

- **Real environment tested**: (A)/(B) drive the **production CLI command
  functions** (`sessionsCompactCommand` and `agentCliCommand`) against a **real
  in-process OpenClaw Gateway server** over a live WebSocket
  (`ws://127.0.0.1:<port>`), with a temporary on-disk session transcript seeded
  with 500 JSONL lines, read back from disk. (C) is split into two parts that
  each cross a real boundary: **(C1) payload source** drives the real Codex
  production function `maybeCompactCodexAppServerSession` against a real
  on-disk session binding (written with `writeCodexAppServerBinding`, read
  back with `readCodexAppServerBinding`) and an injected app-server client, and
  observes the real `thread/compact/start` pending payload; **(C2) CLI display**
  drives the full real `openclaw sessions compact <key>` path (real Commander
  parse → real action → real `sessionsCompactCommand` → real `describeCompaction`
  → real `console.log`) and feeds it that exact pending payload. C2 is a
  display-only proof: only the `callGatewayCli` transport hop is stubbed (the
  gateway handler passes `result.result.details` through verbatim — see
  `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` `respond(...)`), and C1 already
  proves the pending payload is real. (D) drives a real Commander parse of
  `openclaw sessions --store … compact <key>` and captures the real stderr and
  `process.exit` code.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: (A) Started the real
  Gateway, seeded `agent:main:main` with a 500-line on-disk transcript, then
  invoked the production `sessions compact` command function with
  `key: "agent:main:main"`, `maxLines: 50` — full CLI body → callGateway →
  gateway RPC → filesystem path. (B) Invoked the production `agent` command
  function with `--message '/compact'`. (C1) Wrote a Codex app-server binding to
  disk, called `maybeCompactCodexAppServerSession({trigger:"manual", …})`,
  read the binding back from disk. (C2) Ran `openclaw sessions compact
  agent:main:main` through real Commander with the C1 pending payload returned
  by the stubbed transport, and a contrast run returning a genuine no-op. (D)
  Ran `openclaw sessions --store /tmp/other.json compact agent:main:main` and
  `openclaw sessions --all-agents --limit 25 compact agent:work:main` through
  real Commander.

- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted terminal output captured from the real
  command functions on the latest head:

```text
PROOF A — openclaw sessions compact <key>  (real command fn → real gateway over WS)
  url: ws://127.0.0.1:30000
  command stdout (runtime.log):
    Compacted session agent:main:main (kept 50 lines).
    Archived transcript: <tmp>/sess-main.jsonl.bak.2026-06-08T11-08-40.217Z
  command stderr (runtime.error): (none)
  exit code: 0 (process.exit not called = success)
  transcript lines BEFORE compact (on disk): 500
  transcript lines AFTER  compact (on disk): 50
  .bak archive lines (original preserved):   500

PROOF B — openclaw agent --message '/compact'  (real agentCliCommand guard)
  command stderr (runtime.error):
    Slash commands cannot be executed via --message from the CLI.
    Use: openclaw sessions compact <key>
  exit code: 1

PROOF C1 — codex pending payload source  (real maybeCompactCodexAppServerSession, real disk binding)
  on-disk session binding file: <tmp>/session.jsonl
  binding read back from disk (readCodexAppServerBinding):
    {"schemaVersion":1,"threadId":"thread-1","sessionFile":"<tmp>/session.jsonl","cwd":"<tmp>",...}
  codex client request sent: ["thread/compact/start",{"threadId":"thread-1"}]
  returned pending payload:
    {"ok":true,"compacted":false,"result":{"summary":"","firstKeptEntryId":"",
     "tokensBefore":123,"details":{"backend":"codex-app-server","threadId":"thread-1",
     "signal":"thread/compact/start","pending":true}}}

PROOF C2 — codex pending compaction DISPLAY  (real CLI path; only callGatewayCli transport stubbed)
  RPC dispatch reached: callGatewayCli("sessions.compact", {timeout:"180000",json:false}, {key:"agent:main:main"})
  command stdout (real console.log):
    Compaction started for session agent:main:main (pending; completion is reported asynchronously by the backend).
  contrast — genuine no-op (compacted:false, no pending details) stdout:
    No compaction needed for session agent:work:main (already within budget).

PROOF D — reject unsupported inherited parent options  (real Commander parse)
  case 1: openclaw sessions --store /tmp/other.json compact agent:main:main
    stderr: `sessions compact` does not support the parent `sessions` option --store; the gateway resolves the target store from <key> and --agent.
    exit  : 1
  case 2: openclaw sessions --all-agents --limit 25 compact agent:work:main
    stderr: `sessions compact` does not support the parent `sessions` options --all-agents, --limit; the gateway resolves the target store from <key> and --agent.
    exit  : 1

PROOF E — maxLines active-run guard  (real in-process Gateway over WS; committed regression)
  test 1 (no active run): seeded 500-line transcript, maxLines:50
    -> compacted=true, kept=50; on-disk transcript = 50 lines; .bak archive = 500 lines
    -> abortCalls=[], waitCalls=[]            (no run to interrupt)
  test 2 (active embedded run on sess-main): maxLines:50
    -> abortCalls=["sess-main"], waitCalls=["sess-main"]   (same guard as the LLM branch)
    -> then truncated 500 -> 50 on disk
  test 3 (active run cannot be interrupted; wait-for-end returns false): maxLines:50
    -> RPC ok=false BEFORE any archive/truncate; transcript still 500 lines; no .bak created
```

- **Observed result after fix**: (A) The production `sessions compact` command
  printed the success line and `.bak` path, reduced the active transcript from
  500 → 50 lines on disk, and preserved the original 500 lines in the `.bak`
  archive — all through the new CLI command body. (B) `agent --message
  '/compact'` emitted the redirect message on stderr and exited 1. (C) The real
  Codex function produced the `thread/compact/start` pending payload from a real
  on-disk binding (C1), and the real CLI display rendered it as
  `Compaction started for session agent:main:main (pending; …)` while a genuine
  no-op still rendered `No compaction needed …` (C2) — proving the pending
  branch is distinguished from a no-op. (D) Both unsupported-parent-option
  invocations were rejected on stderr with exit 1 before any gateway call. (E)
  With an active embedded run, the maxLines truncate branch fired the same
  abort + wait-for-end interrupt before archiving, then truncated 500 → 50;
  when the run could not be interrupted the RPC failed with the transcript left
  intact at 500 lines and no `.bak` — proving the guard precedes the
  destructive write.

- **What was not tested**: No GUI or screenshot flow. C2 stubs only the
  WS/`callGatewayCli` transport hop (display-only proof); the live transport is
  covered by PROOF A, the gateway handler is verbatim pass-through for
  `result.result.details`, and the pending payload source is covered by C1.

## Tests

- `src/commands/sessions-compact.test.ts` — 6 unit tests: success token delta,
  pending Codex `thread/compact/start` reported as pending (not a no-op),
  `ok:false` → exit 1, RPC throw → exit 1, JSON-mode `ok:false` → payload +
  exit 1, params forwarding (`agentId`/`maxLines`).
- `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts` — parametrized cases proving
  `/compact`, `/compact <text>`, `/compact:<text>`, `/COMPACT`, and
  `  /Compact  ` are all rejected with exit 1 before any gateway or embedded
  turn, while `/compacting …` is NOT mistaken for a control command.
- `src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.test.ts` — regression
  coverage for parent-option handling: a parent `sessions --agent`/`--json` is
  forwarded to `compact`, a compact-level `--agent` overrides the parent, and an
  unsupported inherited parent `--store`/`--all-agents`/`--active`/`--limit` is
  rejected with a non-zero exit (31 tests pass).
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts` — committed coverage for the
  Codex `thread/compact/start` pending payload source backing PROOF C1: the real
  `maybeCompactCodexAppServerSession` returns `ok:true` / `compacted:false` with
  `details.backend = "codex-app-server"`, `details.signal =
  "thread/compact/start"`, and `details.pending = true` for a manual trigger.
- `src/gateway/server.sessions.compaction.test.ts` — three regression tests for
  the maxLines truncate branch over a real in-process Gateway: (1) with no active
  run, `--max-lines 50` truncates a 500-line on-disk transcript to 50 lines and
  preserves all 500 original lines in the `.bak` archive, aborting nothing; (2)
  with an active embedded run, the maxLines branch runs the same
  `interruptSessionRunIfActive` guard as the LLM compact branch — asserting the
  abort + wait-for-end calls fire — **before** archiving and truncating, then
  still truncates 500 → 50; (3) order proof: when the active run cannot be
  interrupted (wait-for-end returns false), the RPC fails **before** any
  archive/truncate — the transcript is left at 500 lines with no `.bak`,
  confirming the interrupt precedes the destructive write. This is the regression
  coverage requested for the active-run guard finding.
